data.title: BME: Defensive Healthcare Exposure With A Tender Offer Opportunity
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (NYSE:BME) is a closed-end fund providing investors exposure to a portfolio of traditional healthcare companies. The fund also then utilizes a covered call strategy to overwrite a portion of its underlying portfolio. This is in an effort to earn additional option premiums to help support its monthly distribution along with harvesting gains from its underlying portfolio. It's also a slightly defensive strategy, which makes the fund a bit more defensive alongside its underlying portfolio.

Since our last update, BME hasn't moved too much in terms of total returns. The discount during this period had widened slightly but has since narrowed back a bit.

CEF discounts/premiums are always important, but this, in particular, is even more important because BME is one of the many BlackRock closed-end funds with a discount management program put in place that can trigger tender offers. For BME, they just recently qualified for the latest round, but it was quite close. That said, the discount is still attractive and having the opportunity to participate in the tender offer could provide some additional upside.

BME Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.38
  • Discount/Premium: -7.50%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.15%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.07%
  • Leverage: N/A
  • Managed Assets: $604.5 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

BME's investment objective is "total return through a combination of income, current gains, and long-term capital appreciation." They will attempt to achieve this by a pretty simple investment policy - "under normal market conditions, at least 80% of its assets in equity securities of companies engaged in the health sciences and related industries and equity derivatives with exposure to the health sciences industry."

Tender Offer Potential

Earlier this year, a number of BlackRock CEFs announced a discount management program, which included BME. This came along with four different measurement periods over the course of the year after the announcement.

If any fund averaged a discount of -7.50% or greater during this period, the fund would conduct a 2.5% tender offer at 98% of NAV. The 2.5% may seem like a small portion, but not everyone participates in these tender offers, meaning it leaves more shares to be repurchased from investors for those who do participate. Given that we have a total of four measurement periods, that also means up to a total of 10% may end up being repurchased through these tenders.

The first tender offers wrapped up in August, and for BME, it worked out particularly well. Here is how I fared with BME during the first round.

BlackRock Health Sciences Trust (BME) saw the best proration I had in this round of offerings, with a total of ~27.27% of shares accepted at a price of $44.4822. BME was already one of my largest positions, but I added even more than what was accepted anyway, thanks to what I view as a very compelling discount that the fund is trading at. I picked the shares up at $41.96.

As we can see, the proration ended up meaning that the number of shares accepted for the tender was nearly 11x the 2.5% tender offer.

With that, the second measurement period results were announced and BME made the list once again. However, it was just barely at an average discount of -7.63% during that period.

The expiration to participate in the tender offer for BME is on November 18. Brokers may have different internal cut-off period, which they should have listed themselves, but if it isn't, then contacting your broker is a good idea.

Now, it's never guaranteed that the second round will be as successful. More investors could participate, or the market could decline sharply, making it less appealing for investors to participate if they do so at a loss. For me, a gain/loss isn't the relevant metric, as the idea would be to repurchase the shares afterward.

Even realizing a loss doesn't mean that I can't sell a small portion for 98% of NAV, which could still be higher than the market price, and then repurchase for lower. That said, I know some investors avoid realizing losses.

The reverse of this, which is also another consideration, is potentially realizing a capital gain in a taxable account. That could make a deal less attractive to consider as well if the discount narrows meaningfully; then, realizing capital gains on top of that could potentially negate any potential opportunity.

The fund's discount has narrowed a bit since this measurement period as well, so this will be one to keep a close eye on heading into the offering. On the other hand, I believe the discount is still attractive, in general, so accumulating a position here has appeal whatever the outcome going forward. As we can see, the fund has traded at premium levels for most of its life, it has only been now more recently (and around the GFC) where that has been coming down.

In general, CEF discounts had been narrowing more broadly, but BME seems to be going in a different direction in terms of its discount/premium.

Monthly Distribution - Modest But Steady

One of the reasons that BME could be seeing its discount widen is that relative to some other healthcare CEF peers, they aren't paying as high of a distribution. That said, personally, I don't mind the 6.15% distribution rate currently as it has been an incredibly steady payout as this is one fund that has never cut its distribution. They paid a quarterly distribution until around 2014, then switched to a monthly distribution but maintained the same equivalent payout.

With a more modest distribution, there comes a lesser degree of risk for a potential distribution cut-even if the fund has to cover it through significant capital gains, as is the case with BME. That is generally the case for most equity-focused CEFs anyway, so BME isn't too different on that front.

Net investment income coverage as of their last semi-annual report came to less than 5%.

With a lower distribution rate, though, it just means they generally have an easier time hitting the ~5.75% NAV distribution rate compared to another healthcare fund like Tekla Healthcare Opportunities Fund (THQ) with its 9.61% NAV rate. Whatever isn't paid out is going to be "retained" by the fund and added to NAV, which could see potential appreciation over time. BME has been able to deliver on that as well, with its NAV coming up close to doubling since its inception.

The tax classifications of the fund's distributions have often been long-term capital gains or return of capital. A small portion was listed as NII, but the portion that was for 2023 was qualified income. Earlier this year, in a prior update, we touched on the tax classifications further:

For tax purposes, the fund has generally characterized the distribution primarily as long-term capital gains and qualified dividends. That's tax-friendly and makes it likely appropriate for holding in a taxable account.

For 2023, we even see some return of capital in the distribution, which can also be beneficial for deferring tax obligations as it reduces one's cost basis. Some of the ROC was destructive in 2023 as healthcare didn't perform the greatest, and that weakness also showed up in BME with a 3.5% total NAV return for the year.

That said, ROC is not a common classification in the fund's distribution either, so over the long term, one probably shouldn't rely on that tax-deferred component.

BME's Portfolio

The fund's latest semi-annual report put turnover at just 9%, which actually puts it on pace to be very low this year relative to prior full fiscal years. For example, 2023 saw turnover come in at 43%, 2022 was at 41%, and 2021 came in at 49%. With that, we haven't seen too much in terms of dramatic shifts this year for the fund's holdings.

The fund is fairly diversified, but a few of the largest holdings do carry a particularly heavy weighting in the fund. That includes UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) as the top two that come to an allocation of nearly 18% by themselves. Boston Scientific Corp (BSX) is also in a fairly hefty position at a nearly 6% weighting, which then pushes the top three positions to account for nearly 24% of the fund's exposure.

In our prior update, these were the same three top positions, but UNH had slid down to around a 7.8% weighting while LLY held the top spot at 9.84%. UNH had a series of unfortunate events that were pushing the price lower- we outlined those in a prior update because it was one of the causes that was pressuring BME significantly. So, we've already seen the negatives that can happen when a fund is carrying such a concentrated position in some underlying names.

That said, since that time, UNH has come roaring back. At one point, it was then LLY having some challenges in terms of its share price, where the shares dropped to a loss for the year. However, that was fairly short-lived, and both UNH and LLY ultimately performed quite well during this 3-month period between updates. UNH slightly outperformed and was able to nab its stop spot back once again in this fund.

Since then, UNH has more recently announced its earnings and shares slipped lower. While the company beat on both the top and bottom line estimates for the latest quarter, guidance for 2025 was a bit weaker than analysts were originally expecting.

Conclusion

With the second discount measurement period passing on the BlackRock discount management program, we once again have a potential opportunity to capitalize on the tender offer. During this latest round, it was quite close as the fund's discount narrowed since the first measurement period. Then, the discount has since narrowed even further.

Whether or not it makes sense to participate in this next tender offer will be something to watch closely. If the discount narrows further, the potential opportunity will also be reduced-with tax liabilities also potentially being a factor for some investors that will need to be taken into consideration as well.

That said, I believe that BME continues to trade at a relatively attractive discount level compared to where it has historically traded. Further, I like the underlying portfolio, so that makes it quite appealing overall, even if the tender offer wasn't available.

data.title: ACV: This Fund Is Now Covering Its Distributions And Is Worthy Of Consideration
data.description:
The Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund (NYSE:ACV) is a closed-end fund that investors can purchase in order to achieve a very high level of current income. The fund certainly manages to do pretty well at this, which is generally the case with most Virtus-run hybrid convertible bond funds. As of the time of writing, the shares of this fund yield 9.96%, which is far higher than the yields of any of the domestic common stock or bond indices:

The yield of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is also higher than that of most of its peers. We can see this here:

We can clearly see that, with the exception of the RiverNorth Opportunities Fund, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has the highest yield among its peers. This is something that could potentially improve the fund's perception in the eyes of potential investors. After all, the higher yield relative to peers means that investors in this fund will receive a higher level of income for a given level of investment. However, the fund's high yield could also be a sign that the market doubts its ability to sustain the payout going forward. The market has a tendency to price the fund's shares at a level that will give it a competitive yield after it cuts to the level that the fund expects it to pay, after all. This may be especially relevant with Virtus funds, as a few of the other convertible bond funds run by this manager have cut their payouts over the past two or three years. We will be sure to have a close look at this over the remainder of this article.

As regular readers can likely remember, we previously discussed the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund in late July of this year. The markets in general have been pretty strong since that time, due to a combination of interest rate cuts by various central banks around the world and the expectations of many investors that generative artificial intelligence will result in a new growth wave that rivaled the Internet expansion of the late 1990s. While I somewhat doubt that artificial intelligence will live up to its hype, this belief has given investors something to believe in and provided capital gains to market participants. As such, we can probably expect that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund will have delivered a fairly respectable return since our last discussion.

This is indeed the case, as shares of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund have appreciated by 5.55% since the previous article was published:

We can, unfortunately, see that this fund failed to keep pace with the S&P 500 Index over the period. This is unsurprising though since a hybrid fund such as this one tends to include both common stocks and bonds. While bonds did pretty well during this period as well (the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index was up 1.06%), the presence of the bonds still drags down the overall performance of the fund. In any case, a 5.55% gain in less than three months is still very reasonable.

However, as I stated in my previous article on this fund:

A simple look at a closed-end fund's price performance does not necessarily provide an accurate picture of how investors in the fund actually did during a given period. This is because these funds tend to pay out all of their net investment profits to the shareholders, rather than relying on the capital appreciation of their share price to provide a return. This is the reason why the yields of these funds tend to be much higher than the yield of index funds or most other market assets.

The distributions represent a real return on investment that is not captured by a chart of the share price performance alone. When we take this into consideration, we get the following alternative to the chart above:

This certainly changes the overall performance picture here. As we can clearly see, investors in the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund actually did better than they would have done simply by buying an S&P 500 Index fund. Everybody likes to beat the market, so this will likely be appealing to most potential investors.

Naturally, though, past performance is no guarantee of future results. We should have a look at this fund's positioning and the industry fundamentals in order to come up with a reasonable projection of where the fund might be in the future. The remainder of this article will focus on analyzing its positioning, finances, and updating our thesis as appropriate.

About The Fund

According to the fund's website, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has the primary objective of providing its investors with a very high level of total return. This makes a lot of sense given the fund's strategy, which is explained on the website:

The Fund seeks to provide total return through a combination of current income and capital appreciation, while seeking to mitigate the risk of capital loss. The Fund strives to dynamically allocate across convertibles, equities, and income-producing securities.

The Fund will normally invest at least 80% of its net assets (plus any borrowings for investment purposes) in a diversified portfolio of convertible securities, income-producing equity securities and income-producing debt and other instruments of varying maturities, of which at least 50% of total managed assets are invested in convertibles. The Fund has the latitude to write covered call options on the stocks held in the equity portion.

Attempts to dampen volatility, relative to an equity-only portfolio, due to the asymmetric risk/return features historically exhibited by convertible securities.

The website does not specifically state how much of the fund must be invested in common equities or in traditional bond debt. However, the 50% allocation to convertibles suggests that it will usually be under 50% allocated to common stocks, and potentially quite a bit less. The phrasing of the second paragraph is such that the fund could be anywhere from 50% to 100% in convertibles, and anywhere from 0% to 50% in common stock or traditional bonds.

The fund's most recent semi-annual report states that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund was holding the following asset allocation as of July 31, 2024:

This is a bit different than what we saw the last time that we looked at this fund. As regular readers may remember, in the previous article, I shared the fund's asset allocation as of April 30, 2024. Here is a summary of the changes from period to period:

The biggest changes that we see here are the reduction in convertible bonds and the increase in equity-linked notes. However, equity-linked notes do have a few things in common with convertible bonds. Investopedia offers the following definition for an equity-linked note:

An equity-linked note is an investment product that combines a fixed-income investment with additional potential returns that are tied to the performance of equities. Equity-linked notes are usually structured to return the initial investment with a variable interest portion that depends on the performance of the linked equity. ELNs can be structured in many different ways, but the vanilla version works like a strip bond combined with a call option on a specific security, a basket of securities or an index like the S&P 500 or DJIA. In the case of a note linked to an equity index, the security would typically be called an equity index-linked note.

Thus, the basic design of an equity-linked note allows bondholders in the company to benefit if the common stock of the same company does well. This is also the big appeal of convertible bonds. The difference between the two, of course, is that holders of an equity-linked note might not technically have the ability to convert their security into the common stock of the issuer. The fact that investors in equity-linked notes do participate in the growth and prosperity of the issuing company allows these securities to provide inflation protection that ordinary bonds lack. It also means that investors can end up getting a return that is far in excess of what investors in straight bonds receive. These are two of the most important reasons why an investor might want to favor convertible bonds over traditional fixed-rate bonds in the current environment.

The fact that equity-linked notes have some similar characteristics to convertible bonds also means that the reduction in the fund's convertible bond position and the corresponding increase in equity-linked notes may not be as significant as it initially appears. After all, the performance characteristics of these two types of securities do have some things in common. The fact that this fund favors convertible securities, equity-linked notes, and even common stocks over convertible bonds should also result in it outperforming most ordinary bond funds over the long term. That is very attractive considering that I strongly suspect that bonds will be a disappointing holding over the long term due to the strong possibility of a resurgence of inflation. Anyone who has been watching the price of gold and silver over the past twelve months is well aware that the market appears to be taking this risk seriously, so income investors should too. Equities are much better at protecting the purchasing power of wealth than fixed-income securities, and convertible bonds (or equity-linked notes) are also better than traditional bonds in such an environment.

In addition to the fund's asset allocation changing somewhat over the past few months, its largest positions have also changed. The fund's website provides the following list of the fund's top ten positions as of September 30, 2024:

The companies on this list are quite a bit different than what we saw the last time that we looked at this fund. Over the June 28, 2024, to September 30, 2024, period, the fund reduced or exited its positions in GS Finance Corporation (both of them), Amazon.com (AMZN), Global Payments, Bank of America Finance, and Barclays (BCS). The newly added positions are NVIDIA (NVDA), Uber Technologies (UBER), PG&E Corporation (PCG), Shift4 Payments (FOUR), Southern Company (SO), and Affirm Holdings (AFRM). Please note that many of these positions are not the common equity in the issuing company, but are rather hybrid securities or corporate bonds. We can see this quite clearly above in the fact that several of these new additions list a coupon rate and a maturity date. As such, the performance will not be identical to that of the common stock that comes up when clicking on one of the ticker symbol links. However, the securities are backed by the financials of the same companies backing the common stock, so curious readers might still wish to take a look.

The fact that there were six changes to the fund's top ten positions list in only a three-month period will almost certainly lead one to conclude that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has a very high turnover rate. This is actually correct, as the semi-annual report states that the fund had a 71% portfolio turnover for the first half of 2024. This works out to 142% annualized, which is certainly on the high side for any type of closed-end fund. This fund historically tends to have a pretty high turnover as well, as we can see from the past few years:

FY 2024

FY 2023

FY 2022

FY 2021

FY 2020

Portfolio Turnover

119%

94%

108%

128%

120%

(all figures are for the twelve-month period ending on January 31of the stated year)

Thus, we can expect that this fund's portfolio will change quite a bit from quarter to quarter and year to year. This also means that the fund will be realizing capital gains fairly often. If its management does a good job with timing, there could be some advantages to this, as it reduces the risk of the fund relying on unrealized gains that can be erased to cover the distribution. However, it is not necessarily the most tax-efficient method to run a fund, but that is irrelevant for someone who is purchasing this fund for their retirement account.

Leverage

As is the case with most closed-end funds, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund employs leverage as a method of boosting the effective yield and total return that it earns from its portfolio. I explained how this works in my previous article on this fund:

Basically, the fund borrows money and then uses that borrowed money to purchase convertible securities, common stock, and other publicly traded securities. As long as the total return that the fund receives from the purchased assets is greater than the interest rate that the fund has to pay on the borrowed money, the strategy works pretty well to boost the effective yield of the portfolio. As this fund is capable of borrowing money at institutional rates, which are considerably lower than retail rates, this will usually be the case.

However, the use of debt in this fashion is a double-edged sword. This is because leverage boosts both gains and losses. As such, we want to ensure that the fund is not employing too much leverage because that would expose us to an excessive amount of risk. I generally do not like to see a fund's leverage exceed a third as a percentage of its total assets for this reason.

According to CEF Data, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund currently has leveraged assets comprising 31.59% of its overall portfolio. This is pretty much in line with the 31.85% leverage that the fund had the last time that we discussed it, which is a bit surprising. After all, as we saw in the introduction, the fund's share price has increased by 5.55% since our last discussion. We would normally expect such an increase to decrease the fund's leverage by a lot more than 26 basis points because it would be expected to accompany a fairly significant increase in the size of the fund's portfolio.

As we can see here, the fund's net asset value increased by 4.91% since our last discussion:

Thus, the fund's borrowings should now represent a smaller proportion of the fund's portfolio than previously. While that is the case, the very small 26 basis point drop in the fund's leverage could suggest that the fund increased its borrowings slightly as the portfolio gained over the period. This fund might be trying to keep its leverage at right around the 31% to 32% level, which would actually match up with the numbers that it has had during our past discussions. That is a leverage level that represents a reasonable balance between the risk and the reward for most strategies, so we probably do not have to worry too much about excessive risk.

However, let us compare this fund's leverage to that of its peers in order to make a further determination:

Fund Name

Leverage Ratio

Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund

31.59%

Bexil Investment Trust

0.00%

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

29.77%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

18.90%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

32.95%

RiverNorth Opportunities Fund

33.08%

(all figures from CEF Data)

We can see that the leverage ratio of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is slightly above the 30.68% median of this peer group. This tells us that the fund's leverage is probably nothing to worry about as long as it keeps its leverage relatively stable.

Distribution Analysis

The primary objective of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is to provide its investors with a very high level of total return. Most closed-end funds aim to deliver the bulk of their investment returns via direct payments to their shareholders as opposed to relying on the capital appreciation of their share price, however. This one is no exception to this, as it pays a monthly distribution of $0.18 per share ($2.16 per share annually). This gives the fund a 9.96% yield at the current share price, which is higher than many of its peers.

The Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has been remarkably consistent with respect to its distribution over the years:

As I observed in a previous article on this fund:

The fund did raise its distribution once, but otherwise, it has been remarkably consistent. This fund's strategy apparently is to pay out an amount that it is fairly confident that it can get from the portfolio and then just pay out any surplus in a special distribution. This works pretty well overall and income-focused investors will probably find a lot to like here. After all, this fund looks like a good way to obtain a regular monthly income that can be used to pay your bills and finance your lifestyle, and it has the advantage of possibly paying a special distribution at the start of the year. It is almost like a job with bonus potential, which is attractive to many retirees.

As is always the case, we want to ensure that the fund can actually afford the distribution that it pays to the shareholders. After all, we do not want the fund to be regularly paying out more than it actually earns from its portfolio and destroying its own net asset value in the process. That is unsustainable over any sort of extended period of time.

The most recent financial report that is available for the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is the semi-annual report for the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024. A link to this document was provided earlier in this article. This is a newer document than was available to us at the time of our previous discussion, so we should have a look at it to check on the fund's coverage.

For the six-month period ending July 31, 2024, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund received $3.756 million in interest and $1.318 million in dividends from the assets in its portfolio. From this total, we subtract the foreign withholding taxes that the fund had to pay to arrive at a total investment income of $5.071 million for the period. The fund paid its expenses out of this amount, which left it with $809,000 available for the shareholders. That was not sufficient to cover the $11.201 million that the fund paid out to its shareholders via distributions.

Fortunately, the fund was able to make up the difference through capital gains. For the six-month period that ended on July 31, 2024, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund reported net realized gains of $12.967 million along with $4.986 million in net unrealized gains. Overall, the fund's net assets increased by $7.709 million after accounting for all inflows and outflows during the period.

We can clearly see that the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund managed to cover all of its distributions over the first six months of its fiscal year, and it did not have to rely on unrealized capital gains to do so. This is a nice change from its failure to do so over the preceding two years. We will certainly want to keep an eye on the fund's finances to make sure that this continues, but I like the improvement here.

Valuation

Shares of the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund are currently trading at a 3.04% discount on net asset value. This is reasonably in line with the 3.29% discount that the shares have had on average over the past month.

Expenses

The Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund has an expense ratio of 3.85%, which will undoubtedly seem rather high to many investors who are used to the sub-1.0% ratios of most passively managed exchange-traded funds. However, a significant proportion of the fund's expenses are simply the interest carrying costs from its leverage. If we exclude these, the fund only had an expense ratio of 1.64%. This is still higher than passive alternatives, but actively managed funds will always be more expensive than passive ones.

Here is how the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund compares with its peers:

Fund Name

Expense Ratio

Expense Ratio ex-Leverage Carrying Costs

Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund

3.85%

1.64%

Bexil Investment Trust

Calamos Strategic Total Return Fund

4.00%

1.53%

Eaton Vance Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

2.60%

1.12%

John Hancock Tax-Advantaged Dividend Income Fund

4.68%

1.22%

RiverNorth Opportunities Fund

3.90%

1.67%

(all figures from CEF Data)

As we can see here, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is far from the most expensive fund out of its peer group. Indeed, the full expense ratio is actually below the median level of 3.875%. If we exclude the leverage carrying costs, there are still two funds that are more expensive than this one in the peer group.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the Virtus Diversified Income & Convertible Fund is one way for investors to achieve a high level of income while remaining invested in assets that are likely to provide superior inflation protection to traditional fixed-income securities. This will likely prove attractive as we enter into an era that is likely to be characterized by high deficit spending and fairly significant amounts of money creation by developed market central banks. This fund has greatly improved since our last discussion, as it did fully cover its distribution over the first half of the fiscal year and had some excess returns to help carry it through the remainder of this year. The fund's expenses might be a sticking point for some, but it is not really out of line with similar closed-end funds, so that is not the end of the world for me. The big concern here is that the fund did fail to cover its distribution during the 2023 and 2024 fiscal years, so we need to make sure that it does not have that problem in the future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: The Coca-Cola Company: Getting A Taste Test Before Earnings
data.description:
For publicly traded companies, there are four times a year that can be incredibly volatile. Each of these involves the release of earnings for each fiscal quarter. This is when new data comes out and, when management obliges, updated guidance is offered. This paints a picture of how the business performed during a three-month window of time and gives the best indications possible as to what the near-term outlook for the company in question might be. It just so happens that, for one of the largest companies in the world, The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE:KO) the next earnings release is just around the corner. On October 23rd, before the market opens, management will be reporting results covering the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year. Leading up to that time, analysts expect a nice increase in profitability. However, revenue is currently forecasted to drop year over year.

This is not the first time that I have written about The Coca-Cola Company this year. In fact, the last article that I published about the company came out late last month. In that piece, I extolled my passion for its hallmark drink. I consume it virtually every day. But as investors, it is imperative that we don't let our own biases control our judgment. And because of how the stock was priced, I ended up rating the firm a 'hold'. Since then, there hasn't been much movement for investors. The stock is down 1.5% compared to the 1.9% increase the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's barely any change at all.

My goal in this article is not to rehash the details of that prior piece. My overall assessment of the business has not changed. Having said that, I do believe that there are important things that investors should be paying attention to for when new data is revealed. In particular, there are certain spots of both strength and weakness that investors would be wise to pay attention to. Ultimately, if the picture can come in better than anticipated, or if shares drop materially in response to the news that is released, my mindset could change. But for now, I'm deciding to maintain a 'hold' rating.

What analysts are forecasting

For the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year, analysts have mixed expectations when it comes to The Coca-Cola Company. For starters, they don't think that revenue will come in all that high. They currently anticipate sales of $11.61 billion. Although that's more money than you, or I could likely fathom, it would actually represent a decline of 2.8% compared to the $11.95 billion the company reported one year earlier. This is interesting because, so far this year, the picture for the business has been quite positive from a sales perspective. Taking a chart from my prior article, you can see revenue and certain profitability metrics for the business above. Year over year, sales for the company grew by 3.1% for the first half of 2024 compared to the same time last year. That took revenue up from $22.95 billion to $23.66 billion.

*Revenues in Millions of Dollars

This marks a continuation compared to how the company has performed in recent years. However, while I did talk about the different market opportunities for the business, I did not dig into each of its operating regions. Doing so is very illustrative. Outside of its miscellaneous corporate operations, The Coca-Cola Company has six different operating segments. The largest of these is its North America business. Considering that Coca-Cola the products originated from North America and that the US itself is an incredibly developed country where product saturation should probably be a thing, growth in recent years has been impressive. Before diving into this year's figures, using results from the prior three years might be a good place to start. From 2021 through 2023, revenue coming from North America shot up from $13.19 billion to $16.77 billion. In fact, during this three-year window of time, the North American operations accounted for 49.5% of the firm's segment operating revenue growth despite accounting for only 33.1% of those sales in 2021.

This kind of growth has come from a combination of factors, including higher pricing and changes in product mix and geographic mix. Interestingly, this growth occurred even as volumes remained virtually flat during this window of time. Outside of the company's Bottling Investments segment, which took up the largest share of capital expenditures, North America has been a source of major cash outflows for the company from a capital expenditure perspective. Over that three-year window of time, $921.8 million, or 19.6%, of the company's capital expenditures were allocated to the continent.

The other major source of growth for the company has been its Latin America operations. This part of the business is smaller than its operations in the EMEA (Europe, Middle East, and Africa) regions and smaller than its Asia Pacific business. Even so, revenue skyrocketed over the three-year window of time by 40.7% from $4.14 billion to $5.83 billion. Despite only accounting for 10.4% of the company's total segment revenue in 2021, the growth that the region brought in for the company was responsible for 38.6% of the firm's overall revenue expansion. Just like the North America business, the Latin America set of operations ended up benefiting significantly from higher prices and changes in product mix and geographic mix. All combined, these factors were responsible for cumulative growth of 35.7% over this window of time. However, this region also exhibited tremendous volume growth. Volume grew by 7% from 2021 to 2022, while growing another 6% from 2022 to 2023. This is in spite of the fact that the company has only allocated $10.3 million toward capital expenditures to the region.

Other parts of the company have increased as well. But none of them have been as impressive as these two regions. The next best performer was the EMEA set of operations, which reported a 12.3% increase in sales. The 9.2% coming from the firm's Global Ventures unit was not too far behind. This was followed by the 9.1% increase in sales coming from the company's Bottling Investments business. That left the Asia Pacific region as the laggard, with growth of only 3.1%. However, this expansion is somewhat deceptive because much of the growth experienced was offset by foreign currency fluctuations. Over this three-year window of time, changes in price, product mix, and geographic mix, increased by an aggregate of 8.2%. Volume grew another 8%.

*Revenues in Millions of Dollars

As I illustrated in the first chart in this article, the 2024 fiscal year has also been a time of expansion for the business. But again, that growth has not been even across the board. The North America business, for instance, was once again a big source of expansion for the company, with revenue jumping by 8.6% year over year. That resulted in an increase in sales from $8.27 billion last year to $8.99 billion this year. Meanwhile, the Latin America business grew to the tune of 15% from $2.76 billion to $3.18 billion. The same factors that contributed to growth for these units from 2021 through 2023 also contributed to growth in the first half of this year compared to the same time last year. In North America, volume was flat. But changes in price, product mix, and geographic mix, resulted in a nine percent expansion. In Latin America, changes in price, product mix, and geographic mix, pushed sales up by 21%. Volume grew another 4%. It was only because of a 10% hit caused by foreign currency fluctuations that sales weren't higher.

Naturally, the other parts of the company saw mixed results. But at first glance, the one that's really surprising is the Bottling Investments part of the firm. Revenue actually fell 15.8% year over year. This was in spite of the fact that volume expanded by 7% and changes in price, product mix, and geographic mix, added 6% to the firm's top line. Some of the weakness was caused by foreign currency fluctuations. But the real hit came from acquisitions and divestitures. This caused a 26% hit because of management's decision to sell off some of its bottling operations. In particular, during the first half of this year, the firm saw $2.91 billion in proceeds, mostly related to the refranchising of its bottling operations that had been classified as held for sale at the end of last year. The firm also received $720 million of proceeds stemming from the sale of its ownership interest in an equity method investment in Thailand.

In all likelihood, it's this kind of activity that will probably result in any sort of drop in sales for the company for the third quarter of the 2024 fiscal year compared to the same time last year. For decades now, The Coca-Cola Company has made it part of its business model to buy up struggling or underperforming bottlers, improving their operations, and then refranchising them.

When it comes to the third quarter, revenue might drop, but analysts are forecasting a rise in profits. They currently believe that earnings per share will come in at around $0.75. That would be an improvement over the $0.71 per share in earnings generated the same time last year. Should this come to fruition, it would translate to a rise in net profits from $3.09 billion to $3.24 billion. We don't know what other profitability metrics will look like. But in the table above, you can see what these were for the same time of the 2023 fiscal year. It is very likely, I would argue, that these metrics will also improve year over year if profitability increases as well.

*Profits in Millions of Dollars

Just like what we have seen from a revenue perspective, earnings and earnings growth for the company have not been evenly spread. It's not hard to imagine which of the two operating segments performed the best during this window of time. Naturally, this would be its North America and Latin America sets of operations. North America reported operating profit growth from $3.33 billion in 2021 to $4.44 billion last year. In Latin America, growth was from $2.53 billion to $3.43 billion. Because there was weakness for some parts of the business, most notably the Asia Pacific region, these profit increases from Latin America and North America ended up being responsible for 86.1% of the company's increase in segment operating profit during these three years.

But when it comes to the 2024 fiscal year, the picture changes to some extent. Latin America continued to be a source of growth for the firm, with segment profits expanding by 12.8% from $1.65 billion to $1.86 billion. This is not surprising to me in the least. And in fact, that growth rate is more than double the growth rate experienced by the four segments of the company that involve specific geographic regions. But the weak spot during this time ended up being North America. Profitability dropped from $2.25 billion to $1.76 billion. That's a plunge of 21.9% year over year. Management said that certain costs were higher, such as marketing expenses, various operating costs, and commodity costs. But the real source of the problem ended up being the firm's sports drink business, BODYARMOR.

*Profits in Millions of Dollars

Back in 2021, The Coca-Cola Company acquired the 85% of BODYARMOR that it did not previously own in exchange for $5.60 billion. At that time, this resulted in the firm booking an $834 million gain on a remeasurement of its minority interest in the business. Unfortunately, however, this did not turn out well. During the first half of this year, management booked a $760 million impairment involving these operations. Certainly, it seems as though the purchase is not living up to expectations. Without this, profitability for the company would have been quite a bit higher. In fact, during the first half of 2024, the $5.59 billion in profits that the company generated did come in lower than the $5.65 billion reported the same time last year. So keeping all else the same, net income would have risen year over year during that window of time had it not been for this impairment. But unfortunately, that's just the way things are.

The reason why I bring up this impairment is because, while the first half of this year has not been particularly great from a profitability or cash flow perspective, it's not crazy to expect this trend to reverse for the third quarter. With the aforementioned impairment now out of the way, I see no reason why the firm's bottom line shouldn't return to growth, even though other factors like rising costs in some parts of the business might still persist. The Latin America business should especially be a source of strength for the company.

At this point, some of you might be asking how much additional growth an industry behemoth with a sizable market share can possibly occur. The good news, at least in the near term, is that the answer is probably a lot. In the table above, you can see certain product categories and management's forecast for growth on a region-by-region basis for the 2024 through 2027 fiscal years. While North America does seem to offer weaker prospects than places like the Asia Pacific region or the EMEA regions, that growth is still solid. But most impressive is Latin America, with three of the six product categories indicating annualized growth greater than 10%. And with The Coca-Cola Company already having a good track record in that region, I fully expect things to go well.

Takeaway

Heading into earnings, I do think investors should be paying careful attention to the various operating segments that make up The Coca-Cola Company. Based on my own observations, both North America and Latin America should help to propel things higher. But this doesn't mean that investors should expect an increase in sales. I do think that profits and cash flows will likely grow year over year. However, because of the aforementioned divestitures, revenue will likely come in weak. If this picture ends up being significantly different from what analysts are expecting, I could very well change my assessment of the firm. But sticking to my previous findings regarding how it's priced and considering that nothing fundamentally has changed since then, including really its price, I believe that maintaining a 'hold' rating is logical.

data.title: IWR: Outperformance Likely In A Lower Rate Environment
data.description:
Introduction

Investors often wonder when to invest in large-cap funds or mid-cap funds. In certain phases of the economic cycle, large-cap may outperform mid-cap funds and vice versa. In this article, we will analyze iShares Russell Mid-Cap ETF (NYSEARCA:IWR) and provide our insights on whether this is the right timing to invest in mid-cap stocks.

ETF Overview

IWR has a portfolio of about 800 U.S. mid-cap stocks. The fund basically tracks the Russell Mid-cap Index and has an expense ratio of 0.19%. This is not low as its counterpart Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 ETF (IVOO) charges a lower expense ratio of 0.10%. The fund has a higher exposure to cyclical and economically sensitive sectors. Therefore, they have a higher downside risk in market turmoil. Fortunately, as inflation recedes, the macroeconomic environment is expected to continue to improve through 2025 and 2026. Therefore, IWR should be able to ride on this tailwind and deliver outperformance. The fund also has a reasonable valuation. Therefore, we have a buy-rating for IWR.

Fund Analysis

A portfolio with high exposure to economically sensitive and cyclical sectors

Let us first look at IWR’s sector allocation. The fund has a high exposure to cyclical and economically sensitive sectors. As can be seen from the table/chart below, industrials, financials, consumer discretionary, real estate, materials, and energy sectors represent about 63.5% of IWR’s portfolio. That is not very far from representing two-thirds of the portfolio. These sectors are either cyclical or economically sensitive sectors. An economic recession or economic boom can have a much greater impact on these sectors than defensive sectors, which represent only about 20.3% of IWR’s portfolio.

IWR’s high exposure to economically sensitive and cyclical sectors means the fund can endure higher downside risk in an economic downturn. As can be seen from the chart below, IWR has typically fallen much harder than the S&P 500 index during market turmoil (e.g. in 2016, 2019, 2020, and 2022).

IWR has underperformed in an elevated rate environment

Mid-cap stocks tend to have inferior balance sheets and less established business models than large-cap stocks. Therefore, mid-cap stocks may be impacted more negatively in a high-rate environment than large-cap stocks. This has been the case when we observe IWR’s fund performance relative to the S&P 500 index, which includes mostly large-cap stocks. As can be seen from the chart below, in the elevated-rate environment in the past 2~3 years, IWR only delivered a total return of 13.0%. In contrast, the S&P 500 index delivered a much better total return of 28.6%.

Macroeconomic environment is becoming favorable for mid-cap stocks

Fortunately, the tide has turned. As can be seen from the chart below, U.S. core inflation has declined considerably from the peak of 6.6% in mid-2022 to only 3.2% in August 2024. This has opened the door for the Federal Reserve to start its rate decline cycle. In fact, Fed fund rate has been reduced by 50 basis points in September and more rate cuts are expected through 2025. This should create a much more favorable macroeconomic environment for mid-cap stocks.

Besides the start of a new rate cut cycle, U.S. composite PMI has been trending higher from the low reached in August 2022 to about 54 in September 2024. For reader’s information, PMI is a forward economic indicator. A reading below 50 means the economy will likely head towards contraction. On the other hand, a reading above 50 means the economy is likely to enter expansion mode. Therefore, we are now seeing a much more favorable macroeconomic environment than 1~2 years ago.

Growth rates are expected to outperform the S&P 500 index

The improving macroeconomic environment should be good news for IWR, and we expect IWR’s portfolio of mid-cap stocks to benefit more than large-cap stocks in the S&P 500 index. Below is a table that shows the consensus earnings growth estimate for S&P 400 mid-cap stocks and S&P 500 large-cap stocks. Although S&P 400 mid-cap stocks are not exactly the same stocks as mid-cap stocks in IWR’s portfolio, both have very similar trends and performances. Hence, the table still provides meaningful comparison between mid-cap and large-cap stocks.

As can be seen from the table below, earnings growth rate for S&P 400 mid-cap index is expected to be negative 2.7% this year, likely due to the high-rate environment. In contrast, large-cap stocks in the S&P 500 index are still able to deliver an expected earnings growth rate of 9.5% in 2024. However, as a new rate cut cycle begins, these mid-cap stocks are expected to deliver earnings growth rates of 16.5% in 2025 and 15.2% in 2026. In contrast, large-cap stocks have inferior growth rates of 14.9% in 2025 and 13.0% in 2026.

Consensus Earnings Growth Forecast

S&P 400 Mid-cap

S&P 500 Large-Cap

Source: Yardeni Research, Created by author (data accessed on Oct. 18, 2024)

Reasonable valuation

Now that we have come to the conclusion that IWR’s earnings growth outlook will likely be better than the S&P 500 index in 2025 and 2026, we will check whether its valuation is reasonable or not. Below is a chart that shows the forward P/E ratio of the S&P 400 mid-cap index. As can be seen from the chart, the forward P/E ratio is currently about 16.1x. In the past 25 years, the valuation has typically been between 14x and 18x. Therefore, the current valuation of 16.1x appears to be fair. As such, we do not think IWR as expensive. In contrast, the S&P 500 large-cap index has a forward P/E ratio of 22.0x. This ratio is only behind the 25x valuation during the Internet-Dot-Com bubble in 2000 and the ratio of about 23.5x during the pandemic in 2020. Therefore, IWR’s valuation appears to be very reasonable, and the S&P 500 index appears to be expensive.

Risk assessment: rate decline may be slower as inflation is difficult to tame

While IWR’s outlook appears to be positive, investors should keep in mind that the process for the Federal Reserve to cut the rate lower may be slow. This is because inflation can be very sticky (e.g. wage growth can lead to higher product/services prices, and this will again lead to demand for higher wages). Therefore, the Federal Reserve may not be able to lower the rate to neutral rate too quickly. Otherwise, they may risk inflation from flaring up. If inflation flares up, the macroeconomic environment will become less favorable for IWR.

Investor Takeaway

As we have discussed in our article, the macroeconomic environment appears to be heading towards the right direction. Therefore, IWR should benefit in this scenario. Given its inexpensive valuation, we think the fund deserves a buy rating.

Additional Disclosure: This is not financial advice and that all financial investments carry risks. Investors are expected to seek financial advice from professionals before making any investment.

data.title: This May Be The Most Expensive Stock Market In 40 Years
data.description:
Let's call it what is - quite possibly, the most expensive stock market in the past 40 years. Yes, the dot-com period saw higher raw valuations, but at least during the dot-com period, you were getting growth that could support those valuations. Based on this, we are paying more for stocks today than ever, and history tells us this doesn’t end well.

While some will argue that the other 493 stocks in the S&P 500 are more fairly valued, and they may be, chances are if you invested in a stock market index fund, it is trading the S&P 500, not the S&P 493.

As a result, today's investors are paying a premium for the index that history tells us may not deliver the expected future returns. This could mean lower future returns for those heavily invested in index funds.

The PE Ratio Is At Historical High

The current valuations of the S&P 500 quickly place it among the most expensive of the past 40 years, including the dot-com years, not just by one measure but by multiple measures. The price-to-sales ratio, the PE ratio, and the dividend yield quickly show that the current market valuation compares and exceeds the valuations witnessed in the dot-com bubble and the Covid bubble.

The price-to-earnings ratio is by far the most used metric for measuring valuation. However, the PE ratio isn't perfect, and just because it is high doesn't mean the market is expensive. The PE ratio alone tells us how much we pay for earnings versus previous periods, giving us hints about market valuation. However, it doesn't tell us if the market is cheap or expensive; it only does when considering the earnings growth rate.

The PE ratio alone shows that blended earnings over the next 12 months, the S&P 500 trades at 23 times earnings, versus about 25 times earnings in 1999 and 2000, and 22 times in 2020 and 2021. However, it is also essential to consider the earnings growth rates and apply them to the PE ratio because, in 1999 and 2000, earnings grew by around 15% to 17% per year. In 2020 and 2021, earnings growth peaked at around 40% as they recovered from the COVID-19 lows. By the summer of 2024, earnings growth had peaked around 13%.

When adjusting the PE ratio for the growth rate, we find that in the 2000 time frame, the PEG ratio was around 1.5; in 2021, it was around 0.5. Today, that ratio is around 1.6, which means that when adjusting today's PE ratio for its growth rate, the market is more expensive than that of the dot-com period.

The other factor is that the PE ratio will tend to expand and contract with the underlying earnings growth rate. This would suggest that if the earnings cycle has experienced peak growth, the PE ratio should experience multiple contraction as growth continues to slow. The latest evidence suggests that the PE growth rate will likely decrease.

Looking forward, we can see that the growth rate is expected to fall to around 11% in 2026 from around 13.6% in 2025. For earnings, this would mean that for the PEG ratio to remain at 1.6, the PE multiple would need to contract to 17.6 by 2026. At 2026 estimates of $305 per share, the S&P value would decline to 5,368. Of course, this assumes that 2026 earnings estimates remain static and do not drop. However, given that earnings estimates for 2024 and 2025 are falling, it is highly probable that 2026 estimates should drop as well over time to adjust for lower earnings presently.

The Price-to-Sales Comparison

The same is true when looking at the price-to-sales multiple for the S&P 500, which currently stands at 3, higher than in 2021 when it reached 2.9 and higher than in April 2000 at 2.7. Again, just like earnings, sales growth today is much slower than in the past.

Sales growth was around 20% in early 2001, but that growth melted quickly as the US economy headed toward recession, leading to a falling price-to-sales ratio.

In 2021 and early 2022 sales growth peaked around 16%, and then started to head lower as the Fed raised rates to slow inflation.

Today, sales growth peaked at 6%. Meanwhile, the price-to-sales ratio has climbed to 3, which marks a high point over the last 40 years or is near a higher point. This is amazing, given that sales growth is only a fraction of what was compared to previous periods of higher multiples.

Sales growth is expected to remain flat for 2025 and 2026, meaning there’s little chance for growth to accelerate. In comparison, the growth-adjusted price-to-sales ratio was around 0.135 in 2000 and 0.181 in 2021, and today, it stands at 0.5—indicating that on a growth-adjusted basis, the market is much more expensive than in the past. With the current price-to-sales ratio around three and expected sales growth at 6% in 2026, maintaining a growth-adjusted ratio of 0.5 would value the S&P 500 at approximately 6,330, based on projected revenue of $2,110 per share.

However, it is unclear how likely that is to happen, considering that historically, the price-to-sale ratio tends to expand and contract with the revenue growth rate over time. One would think that with the sales growth rate remaining flat over time, the multiple would begin to contract. It is especially worth considering that the median Price-to-sales ratio is closer to 1.7; at that ratio, the S&P 500 is valued at just 3,600.

The Dividend Yield

Even the dividend yield is among the lowest of all time, only lower during the dot-com bubble when it reached a low of 1.05%. When creating a ratio of the S&P 500 dividend yield versus the 10-year Treasury, it is clear that the S&P 500 is today only more expensive than during the period following the 1987 crash and the dot-com bubble.

It is always possible for the dividend yield to fall further, and if it were to fall to the lows seen in 2000 at 1.05%, at a current dividend rate of $74.40, the S&P 500 would rise to around 7,100, which would push the ratio of the dividend yield versus the 10-year to around 3.9. However, the median ratio appears closer to 2.2, meaning the dividend yield rises to around 1.85%, placing the dividend yield at a level closer to where it has historically traded since 2002. At the current dividend of $74.40 and 1.85% dividend yield, it would value the S&P 500 at roughly 4,000

There are plenty of easy ways to assume the market goes higher from current levels, assuming multiples continue to expand. More complicated is figuring out whether those multiples can continue to expand. If estimates for future growth rates are correct and human psychology hasn't changed, then multiples shouldn't expand. History tells us that they should contract.

History also tells us that we are paying a lot for future earnings growth compared to what we have spent in the past, even when compared to the dot-com bubble.

Does it mean that one shouldn't own stock? No, it doesn't mean that at all. It just means that stock selection today, depending on one's time frame, really matters because if you are a buy-and-hold investor or an investor in an index fund that invests in stocks, you are paying a lot to own stocks.

History tells us that we should expect lower future returns.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Tectonic Therapeutic: Interesting CV Disease Drug Developer
data.description:
Tectonic Therapeutic (NASDAQ:TECX) went up over 100% on positive phase 1 data of its lead candidate TX45 targeting Group 2 Pulmonary Hypertension in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), or PH-LHD. That was in September. A month earlier, the company announced the beginning of a phase 2 trial which should read out in 2026. They also have an open label IV study ongoing with 0.3 mg/kg and 1 mg/kg doses, and is now being dosed at 3 mg/kg following a good tolerability profile, which will topline in 2025.

Tectonic develops molecules targeting the G-protein coupled receptors (GPCRs). GPCRs are a large family of cell surface receptors which include such widely known names like GLP-1, 5-HT and Beta-adrenergic receptors, the latter of which is targeted by beta-blockers for cardiovascular diseases, including PH, which is basically high blood pressure of the arteries in the lungs.

Tectonic has a bit of history. A few years ago, we used to know it as Avrobio, which used to develop gene therapies for Fabry and Gaucher diseases. Avrobio did not fare well, and last year sold parts of its pipeline to Novartis (NVS) for $87.5mn. In January this year, Tectonic reverse merged with Avrobio to form the current company in order to develop Tectonic’s pipeline. This was also a quick way to get on NASDAQ for the privately held Tectonic. This history is probably irrelevant to the current Tectonic story, whose pipeline has no relation to Avrobio’s old pipeline.

GPCR and TX45’s indication

TX45 is an Fc-relaxin fusion molecule that activates the RXFP1 receptor, the GPCR target of the hormone relaxin. The relaxin portion of the molecule binds to the RXFP1 or relaxin family peptide receptor 1, activating intracellular signaling cascades that lead to relaxation of blood vessels (vasodilation), which has direct benefit for hypertension. The Fc portion of the fusion molecule consists of the constant region of an antibody (immunoglobulin). It helps extend the molecule’s half life, improves pharmacokinetics and increases its stability. This is important because while natural relaxin is rapidly cleared by the body, TX45 is retained long enough to extend its therapeutic effect.

The following quote from the company material will help understand how relaxin became identified for its therapeutic benefit:

Relaxin is an endogenous peptide hormone, expressed at low levels in both men and women. In normal human physiology, relaxin is upregulated during pregnancy where it exerts vasodilative effects, reduces systemic and pulmonary vascular resistance and increases cardiac output to accommodate the increased demand for oxygen and nutrients from the developing fetus.

Relaxin also exerts antifibrotic effects on pelvic ligaments to facilitate delivery of the baby. It has long been hypothesized that these unique dual aspects of relaxin biology may offer therapeutic potential in the treatment of cardiovascular disease.

Group 2 PH is a type of PH caused by left-sided heart disease. This has 3 subtypes, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF), heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF), and valvular heart disease (VHD). There are over 600,000 Americans with Group 2 PH with HFpEF, the target of TX45.

There are very few approved treatment options for Group 2 PH with HFpEF. Drugs approved for PAH (pulmonary arterial hypertension) are not effective against Group 2 PH with HFpEF, and may even be harmful. There are no specific approved drugs targeting the intersection of PH and HFpEF, and it is currently treated with drugs that target various aspects of heart failure, including diuretics to address fluid retention, ACE inhibitors, ARBs, and beta-blockers to treat HFpEF, and other emerging heart failure drugs.

Merck’s (MRK) sotatercept, in studies for Group 1 PH or PAH, is also being studied for Group 2 PH. Tenax Therapeutics (TENX) is developing Levosimendan for Group 2 PH. AstraZeneca (AZN) is developing AZD3427, a relaxin receptor agonist and “a selective long‐acting analog of relaxin,” in phase 2b studies targeting Group 2 PH. This study will be completed next year.

Financials

TECX has a market cap of $551mn and a cash reserve of $185mn. Research and development expenses were $7.1 million for the three months ended June 30, 2024, while general and administrative expenses were $4.3 million. At that rate, they have a cash runway of 12+ quarters. The company estimates that their cash will last into 2027.

Risks

TX45 is targeting an area of unmet medical need, so it is well-positioned. However, first, it is very early stage, and there is no efficacy data. And second, AZN, a much bigger company, has a similar molecule in a similar stage, so the competitive space is constricted.

Bottom Line

I like TECX. It is relatively unknown, Wells Fargo has good things to say about it, comparing the lead molecule to Novartis’ serelaxin. Cash runway is long, and while there is no real patient data yet, I believe this one might be a keeper. I will consider taking a pilot position ahead of data next year.
